lines.text: Welcome back to hour two. The clock reads {{CLOCK}} Madison time. We're {{ELAPSED}} into the Purge.
And what an opening gambit it's been. The early-night kill count is tracking at 19 confirmed. That's 11 more than this point last year.
Visibility is degrading. We're getting reports of multiple structure fires — the smoke's visible from the Beltline.
Which is interesting because the wind pattern hasn't shifted significantly. Southwest 8 miles per hour, which means the fires are concentrated on the West Side or Capitol perimeter.
West Side. Three separate reports in the last eight minutes. Hilldale area.
The wealthy are castling early. Moving assets, securing perimeters. It's a defensive posture that typically doesn't activate until hour four.
Which means they're responding to something we haven't indexed yet.

Or they've accessed intelligence about coordinated activity in their neighborhoods. The West Side has historically been statistically secure — the private security density is the highest in the metro.
Holden.
Right. What I'm saying is: something changed.
The fires are real. The smoke is real. Whether that signals a threat shift or opportunistic arson remains to be seen.
I ran a comparative analysis against the 2164, 2165, and 2166 fire-initiation timelines. In all three years, structure fires didn't appear until hour four. Never before hour three.
We're in hour two.
We are in hour two, and we have fires. Which suggests either a coordinated incendiary action or a copycat cascade from a single initial ignition.
You're speculating.
I'm extrapolating from available data. The methodology is sound.

The temperature at our last reading was 41 degrees Fahrenheit. Humidity at 67 percent. NWS forecast models — even though they're not actively updating — predicted no precipitation until the 23:00 hour.
Which means the fires will propagate more readily. The wood won't be dampened. The air will carry embers across block lines.
Which is why we're seeing smoke across the isthmus. The fires are spreading geographically as well as temporally.
This is actually consistent with a middle-game exchange pattern in chess — the players are no longer in the opening. They're trading pieces rapidly. The board is becoming chaotic.
Holden, we're not playing chess.
No, but the principles apply. The early game is cautious. The middle game is where you commit resources. You accept losses to gain positional advantage. You —
Holden. Purge. Right now.
Right. Yes. The point is: we're accelerating into the violent phase faster than the historical model predicts, and that's a data point that matters.
East Side remains relatively calm. Atwood area is quiet. Schenk's Corners — no new reports in the last ten minutes.
The East Side is the historical hotspot, but it has a rhythm. It follows a pattern. The West Side fires are pattern-breaking.
Which might mean organized activity, or it might mean coincidence.
Coincidence is statistically unlikely at this temporal clustering.
Everything's unlikely until it happens. That's why we're here.
Fair point. I apologize for the abstract analogies. I know you prefer direct language.
I prefer accuracy. The fires exist. The smoke exists. The West Side residents are responding to a threat they perceive as imminent.
Perceived or actual?
Does it matter?
It does to them.
Exactly. So we report what's observable. The fires. The smoke. The evacuation responses.
And we don't speculate about coordination.
We don't.
Even though the data suggests —
Holden.

Okay. Moving on. The kill count remains at 19. That's our baseline for this hour. East Side accounts for 8 of those. Capitol Square area accounts for 4. Williamson Street, 3. The remaining 4 are distributed across secondary neighborhoods.
Which is a flatter distribution than last year. Last year the East Side had 12 of the first 19.
Exactly. Which suggests that either the threat distribution has shifted or the participants are spreading geographically from the start.
Or the data we're receiving is incomplete.
Always a possibility. Our sources are real-time but not comprehensive. We're missing some reporting from the neighborhoods without cellular coverage or active field observers.
North Side is historically underreported.
North Side is also historically the lowest-kill area in the metro. Last year it was 9 total for the entire twelve hours.
Which is anomalous for the city's demographics.

Exactly. The North Side has the highest concentration of single-family dwellings with fortified basements. The cost of entry for a barricade is lower. The density of organized community defense is higher.
Meaning people there are more likely to stay home.
Or they're more organized about defending in place. Either way, the outcome is fewer deaths. Which is statistically anomalous but socially predictable.
Visibility update: smoke is now visible from the State Street area. Multiple sources.
State Street is a fire hazard by definition. The structures there are salvage, mostly. Pre-restoration ruins. If a fire starts on State Street, it will spread without resistance.
Are you saying State Street is going to burn?
I'm saying State Street is vulnerable to catastrophic fire propagation if someone with incendiary capability operates there. And we're seeing fires active. So statistically —
You're speculating again.
I'm scenario-modeling based on historical precedent and current conditions.
Model it privately. Report facts on air.
The fact is: we have active fires and reduced visibility. Both conditions correlate with increased casualties in past Purges. That's a fact.
Fair. That's reportable.
It's also worth noting that reduced visibility actually suppresses some types of Purge activity. People with firearms can't engage effectively in smoke. It might be a net neutral effect.
Or it might be a net positive for people trapped in those smoke zones trying to get to shelter.
Yes. Also true.
We should probably mention that to anyone still listening who's in the Hilldale or Capitol Square area. Visibility is poor. Use caution moving between locations.
And stay aware of smoke inhalation symptoms if you're sheltering in place.
Shortness of breath, chest pain, stinging eyes.
If you're experiencing any of those, open a window if it's safe to do so. Get low — smoke rises. And if you have a mask rated for particulates, use it now.
We're {{ELAPSED}} into the Purge. Twelve more hours ahead. The fires are contained to the West Side and Capitol perimeter as of this report.
But spreading. The wind is pushing the smoke south and east. That's important information.
Holden, how many structure fires have we logged in the two hours since 19:00?
Seven confirmed structure fires. Thirteen vehicle fires. The vehicle fires are concentrated on the Beltline, which is typical for this hour — people trying to flee in vehicles, getting intercepted.
And the structure fires?
All on the West Side. Two in Hilldale, three near the Capitol, two near Midvale.
Which supports your theory about coordinated incendiary activity.
I'm not supposed to speculate.
I'm asking you directly. Does the data pattern suggest coordination?

The temporal clustering and geographic distribution are consistent with a coordinated action, yes. But they're also consistent with copycat behavior from a single initial ignition that spread through communication networks.
Which is more likely?
Statistically? Copycat. People are responsive to observed behavior. Someone sets one fire, others set fires. It's a cascade, not a conspiracy.
But you're not certain.
I'm never certain. I'm confident in the analysis, not the conclusion.
That's fair. That's honest.
I try.
The clock reads {{CLOCK}}. Two hours in. We'll continue monitoring the West Side fire situation and update as new reports arrive.
And we'll have neighborhood-specific updates at the top of hour three. Quinn will be back with the anchor desk at 21:00.
Right now, if you're sheltering in place on the West Side, stay aware of the air quality around you. Keep windows closed if you don't see active fire nearby. Stay tuned to this frequency.
And if you're in a vehicle on the Beltline or any main evacuation corridor, avoid the 90 minutes that typically see the highest vehicle-based activity. We're not there yet, but it's coming.
That's our two-hour report. Holden, do you have final numbers?

Final confirmed count for hour two: 19 deaths. 12 serious injuries. 7 structure fires, 13 vehicle fires. East Side remains the primary activity zone, but threat distribution is broader than last year at this time.
We'll update again at 21:00. Stay safe out there. PRGE broadcasts until 07:00.

tickerItems.text: Hour 2 confirmed kills: 19 total. East Side 8. Capitol Square 4. Williamson St 3. Secondary neighborhoods 4.
SMOKE ALERT: Multiple structure fires reported West Side and Capitol perimeter. Visibility reduced isthmus-wide. Residents: close windows, use particulate masks if available. Air quality degrading.
FIRE COUNT: 7 structure fires (Hilldale, Capitol, Midvale). 13 vehicle fires (Beltline concentration). Wind pushing smoke south and east. Expect visibility reduction to spread.
Temperature holding 41°F. Humidity 67%. No precipitation expected until 23:00 hour. Wind SW 8 mph. Fire propagation conditions optimal.
